metadata.description: Comprehensive exercise graph dataset for gym workout builder app. 162 exercises across 7 files with full graph connectivity (substitutes, complements, superset candidates). Beginner-friendly with video references. Generic equipment names — works at any well-equipped commercial gym.
file_structure: 00_schema_and_metadata.json — This file. Schema, equipment, muscle groups, templates, graph edge semantics.
01_legs_quads_glutes.json — 30 exercises. Squat variations (barbell, smith, hack, pendulum, belt), lunges, split squats, leg press, leg extensions, hip thrusts, glute bridges, adduction/abduction.
02_legs_hamstrings_calves.json — 19 exercises. Deadlift variations (conventional, sumo, trap bar, RDL), leg curls, nordic curls, calf raises, kettlebell swings, back extensions.
03_chest.json — 25 exercises. Bench press variations (flat, incline, decline, close-grip, floor press), flyes (dumbbell, cable, machine), dips, push-up variations, hex press, pullovers.

04_back.json — 24 exercises. Rows (barbell, dumbbell, cable, machine, Pendlay, seal, T-bar, landmine), pull-ups/chin-ups (overhand, neutral, band-assisted), pulldowns (wide, close, reverse grip, single-arm), pullovers.

05_shoulders.json — 20 exercises. Overhead presses (barbell, dumbbell, Arnold, machine, smith), lateral raises (dumbbell, cable, machine), face pulls, rear delt work, front raises, shrugs, prone Y-raises.

06_arms.json — 24 exercises. Bicep curls (barbell, EZ, dumbbell, hammer, preacher, incline, bayesian, spider, cable, concentration, reverse), tricep extensions (pushdown, overhead, skull crusher, kickback, dip), forearm/grip work (wrist curls, farmer's walk, dead hang).

07_core_and_functional.json — 20 exercises. Abs (cable crunch, hanging raises, decline crunch), anti-extension (plank, ab wheel, dead bug, bird dog, hollow body), anti-rotation (Pallof press), rotation (Russian twist, woodchop), anti-lateral flexion (side plank, suitcase carry), plyometrics (box jump, squat jump), conditioning (mountain climbers, battle ropes).
metadata.video_source: muscleandstrength.com — each link has a short video demo plus written form cues. All free, no login needed.

v2_to_v3: Added 12 exercises across files 01, 03, 04, 05 to fill equipment and movement pattern gaps
Added 4 exercises to file 06 (bayesian curl, spider curl, reverse curl, single-arm overhead extension) for complete bicep length-tension spectrum and forearm coverage
Added 5 exercises to file 07 (hanging knee raise, bird dog, hollow body hold, squat jump, suitcase carry) for complete core training paradigm and progression gap coverage
Fixed all broken graph references (0 remaining), eliminated all generic 'any_*' references
Added 63 cross-file superset and complement edges for antagonist pairing and synergist relationships
Added edge_semantics section defining how substitutes, complements, and superset_candidates should be used by the app
Added 6 new equipment types, expanded progression chains, added new scientific principles
Updated all file descriptions and exercise counts

how_to_use_this_data.for_beginners: Each exercise has a difficulty rating (beginner/intermediate/advanced) and a 'beginner_tips' field with plain-English form cues. Start with 'beginner' exercises. The 'substitutes' array gives easier or alternative options.
how_to_use_this_data.workout_position: Exercises are tagged with when to do them: 'early' = do first when fresh (big compound lifts), 'late' = do last (small isolation moves). Follow this order for safety and performance.
how_to_use_this_data.push_vs_pull: Every exercise is tagged 'push', 'pull', or 'isometric'. Balance pushing and pulling volume roughly 1:1 across your training week to prevent imbalances and shoulder injuries.
how_to_use_this_data.substitutes: If a machine is taken or unavailable, check the 'substitutes' array for alternatives that work the same muscles with the same movement pattern.
how_to_use_this_data.complements: The 'complements' array lists exercises that pair well in the SAME workout session — synergists and same-muscle-group variations. Use these to build out a training day.
how_to_use_this_data.superset_candidates: The 'superset_candidates' array lists exercises to do back-to-back during rest periods — typically antagonist pairs (bench ↔ row) or non-competing fillers (planks between squat sets).
how_to_use_this_data.video_url: Click the link for a short video demo with written instructions.
how_to_use_this_data.antagonist_pairing: Use the muscle_groups.antagonist field to derive push/pull pairings at the template level. The app doesn't need every row to explicitly list every bench press — it derives antagonist sessions from muscle group metadata.

edge_semantics.overview: The three edge types serve different purposes in workout generation. Understanding these distinctions is critical for building coherent programs across different training splits.

substitutes.relationship: Same muscle group, same movement pattern, similar difficulty
substitutes.use_case: Equipment unavailable, user preference, injury workaround
substitutes.direction: Bidirectional — if A substitutes for B, B substitutes for A (usually)

complements.meaning: These belong in the SAME session when training the same muscle group
complements.relationship: Synergists, same-group variations, exercises that cover different portions of a muscle
complements.use_case: Building a push day, pull day, leg day, etc. These are exercises that train together.
complements.direction: Generally symmetric within muscle groups, plus cross-file synergist links (curls complement rows, pushdowns complement bench)
complements.example: barbell_bench_press complements incline_dumbbell_press (same session, different angle) and tricep_pushdown (synergist isolation after compound)
complements.not_for: Antagonist pairing. Rows do NOT complement bench press — they belong in different sessions on a push/pull split. Use muscle_groups.antagonist for cross-group pairing at the template level.

superset_candidates.meaning: Do these BACK-TO-BACK during rest periods for time efficiency
superset_candidates.relationship: Antagonist pairs, non-competing fillers, or same-station convenience pairs
superset_candidates.use_case: User wants to superset exercises. App suggests pairings that don't interfere with each other.
superset_candidates.direction: Intentionally asymmetric in some cases. Core exercises list compounds as superset targets (planks between squat sets), but compounds don't list core back — because compounds aren't filler for core work.
superset_candidates.example: barbell_bench_press ↔ barbell_row (antagonist), plank → barbell_bench_press (non-competing filler, one-directional)
superset_candidates.array_size: Capped at ~6-8 per exercise. High-traffic exercises like face_pull and leg_curl_lying are referenced by many more exercises than they list back — this is intentional trimming, not a bug.

app_routing_logic.push_pull_legs: Use 'complements' to build each session. All exercises in a push session complement each other. Antagonist relationships are implicit — push day and pull day are separate.
app_routing_logic.upper_lower: Use 'complements' for within-group ordering. Use muscle_groups.antagonist to pull from both push and pull groups into one upper session. 'superset_candidates' for efficient pairing within the session.
app_routing_logic.full_body: Use muscle_groups.antagonist to ensure balanced coverage. Use 'superset_candidates' heavily — full body sessions benefit most from antagonist supersets for time efficiency.
app_routing_logic.supersets: Use 'superset_candidates' directly. These are curated for equipment compatibility and non-competing muscle groups.

exercise_schema.name: Human-readable name with equipment/variation in parentheses
equipment: array of equipment needed — all generic names matching equipment_types keys
primary_muscles: main muscles worked — matching muscle_groups keys
exercise_schema.workout_position: early | early_mid | mid | mid_late | late — see workout_position_guide
exercise_schema.bilateral: true = both sides at once, false = one side at a time (unilateral)
exercise_schema.rep_range_hypertrophy: recommended reps for muscle growth (string, may include 'per side' or time-based)
exercise_schema.beginner_tips: plain-English form cues for someone new to the exercise
substitutes: exercise IDs — same pattern, same muscles, use instead
complements: exercise IDs — same session synergists, see edge_semantics
superset_candidates: exercise IDs — antagonist or non-competing back-to-back pairs, see edge_semantics
exercise_schema.notes: additional context, common mistakes, scientific references, pro tips

chest.what_it_does: Pushes things away from your body, brings arms together in front of you

upper_back.what_it_does: Pulls things toward your body, helps with posture

shoulders.what_it_does: Raises arms in all directions, stabilizes the shoulder joint
shoulders.notes: Front delts are synergists for chest pressing. Rear delts are synergists for back pulling. Side delts are only directly trained by abduction (lateral raises).

traps.what_it_does: Shrugs shoulders up, helps hold heavy things

biceps.what_it_does: Bends the elbow, turns palm face-up (supination). Brachialis sits under the bicep and adds arm thickness.

triceps.what_it_does: Straightens the elbow, helps with all pushing. Long head crosses the shoulder — needs overhead work to fully train.
triceps.synergist_for: All pressing movements (bench press, overhead press, dips)

forearms.what_it_does: Grips things, bends and extends the wrist. Trained indirectly by all pulling exercises.

quadriceps.what_it_does: Straightens the knee, helps you stand up from sitting

hamstrings.what_it_does: Bends the knee and extends the hip. Biarticular — needs BOTH hip hinge exercises (RDLs) and knee flexion exercises (leg curls) for complete development.

glutes.what_it_does: Extends the hip (standing up, climbing), stabilizes pelvis. Glute med prevents knee cave during squats.

calves.what_it_does: Points toes down, pushes off when walking/running. Gastrocnemius (straight knee) and soleus (bent knee) need different exercises.

core.what_it_does: Stabilizes your spine, transfers force between upper and lower body. Trained through 6 paradigms: spinal flexion, anti-extension, anti-rotation, active rotation, anti-lateral flexion, and hip flexion.

adductors.what_it_does: Squeezes legs together, stabilizes the knee

abductors.what_it_does: Pushes legs apart, stabilizes hips when walking

equipment_types.barbell: Standard Olympic barbell (45 lbs / 20 kg). The long bar you load plates onto.
equipment_types.dumbbell: Individual weights you hold in each hand. Available in many sizes.
equipment_types.ez_bar: Shorter curved bar that's easier on the wrists for curls and extensions.
equipment_types.cable_machine: Machine with adjustable pulleys and a weight stack. Very versatile — different attachments change the exercise.
equipment_types.smith_machine: Barbell on a fixed vertical track. Good for solo lifting without a spotter.
equipment_types.leg_press_machine: Seated machine where you push a platform away with your legs. 45-degree and horizontal (seated) variants.
equipment_types.hack_squat_machine: Angled machine where you squat with your back against a pad.
equipment_types.pendulum_squat_machine: Lever-arm squat machine with an arc path. Excellent quad isolation with minimal balance demands.
equipment_types.belt_squat_machine: Machine that loads weight from a belt at the hips — zero spinal compression. Great for back issues.
equipment_types.leg_extension_machine: Seated machine that works quads by straightening your legs.
equipment_types.leg_curl_machine: Machine that works hamstrings by bending your knees (lying or seated versions).
equipment_types.chest_press_machine: Seated machine for pressing — like a bench press but guided. Some gyms have incline variants.
equipment_types.lat_pulldown_machine: Cable machine with a wide bar for pulling down toward your chest. Multiple grip attachments available.
equipment_types.seated_row_machine: Seated cable or plate-loaded station for rowing.
equipment_types.pec_deck_machine: Machine for chest flyes — arms come together in front of you.
equipment_types.reverse_fly_machine: Same as pec deck but reversed — arms go behind you for rear delts.
equipment_types.pullover_machine: Nautilus-style machine for lat isolation through shoulder extension. Not in every gym.
equipment_types.lateral_raise_machine: Seated machine with pads for strict lateral raises. Not in every gym.
equipment_types.hip_adduction_machine: Seated machine that squeezes legs together (inner thigh).
equipment_types.hip_abduction_machine: Seated machine that pushes legs apart (outer hip/glute med).
equipment_types.calf_raise_machine: Machine for standing or seated calf raises.
equipment_types.assisted_pull_up_machine: Counterweight machine that reduces bodyweight for pull-ups and dips. More weight = more assistance.
equipment_types.pull_up_bar: Overhead bar for pull-ups and chin-ups.
equipment_types.adjustable_bench: Bench that can be set to incline, flat, or decline.
equipment_types.preacher_curl_bench: Angled pad for strict bicep curls.
equipment_types.ab_wheel: Small wheel with handles for rollout exercises. Cheap, portable, brutally effective.
equipment_types.kettlebell: Cast iron weight with a handle. Used for swings, carries, and goblet squats.
equipment_types.resistance_band: Elastic bands for warmups, assistance, and added resistance.
equipment_types.trap_bar: Hexagonal bar you stand inside of. Also called a hex bar.
equipment_types.medicine_ball: Weighted ball for throwing and core work.
equipment_types.battle_ropes: Heavy ropes for cardio and conditioning.

workout_position_guide.early: Do FIRST (exercises 1-2). Big heavy compound lifts. Need full energy and fresh nervous system.
workout_position_guide.early_mid: Do SECOND (exercises 2-3). Still demanding but can follow the main lift.
workout_position_guide.mid: MIDDLE of workout (exercises 3-5). Moderate effort. Compounds or heavy isolations.
workout_position_guide.mid_late: AFTER compounds (exercises 4-6). Transitioning to lighter accessory work.
workout_position_guide.late: Do LAST. Small isolation, finishers, abs, calves, grip work. Safe when fatigued.

progression_chains.description: Ordered sequences from easiest to hardest within a movement pattern. Use these to suggest the next exercise when a user masters their current level. Each chain represents a clear difficulty or loading progression.

chains.notes: Leg press and smith machine are parallel options (both easier than free barbell), not strictly sequential.
Push-up variations build baseline. Machine bridges to free weights.
Machine and smith machine are parallel beginner options.
Standing barbell press is hardest — requires full core stability.
Chin-ups are easier than pull-ups due to bicep involvement. Neutral grip is between the two.
Supported rows (machine, chest-supported) before unsupported. Pendlay row demands the most back strength and hip hinge control.
Cable pull-through teaches the pattern. Trap bar is the safest loaded hinge. Conventional and sumo are parallel — body proportions determine which suits a lifter.
Reverse lunge is easier on knees than forward. Bulgarian is the most demanding due to balance and single-leg loading.
Glute bridge is bodyweight foundation. Smith machine simplifies barbell setup.
Dead bug and bird dog are warmup-level. Ab wheel rollout is advanced — master 60-second planks first.
Cable crunch allows progressive overload. Hanging variations progress through bent-knee to straight-leg.
Pallof press (anti-rotation) is safest. Russian twist involves spinal rotation under load — caution with back issues.
Side plank is isometric. Suitcase carry adds dynamic anti-lateral flexion under heavy load while walking.
Always do plyometrics when fresh (beginning of workout). Step down from box jumps, don't jump down.

full_body_beginner_3x_week.description: Best starting point. Hit every muscle 3x per week. Rest a day between sessions. ~45-60 minutes per session.
full_body_beginner_3x_week.session: Squat variation → Bench or Press → Row variation → Pulldown or Pull-Up → Leg Curl → Face Pull → Calf Raise → Abs
full_body_beginner_3x_week.superset_opportunities: Face pulls between pressing sets. Calf raises between squat sets. Abs between any compound.
full_body_beginner_3x_week.notes: Start with beginner-difficulty exercises from each pattern. Progress through progression_chains as movements are mastered.

upper_lower_4x_week.description: Great intermediate split. Alternate upper and lower body across 4 days (e.g., Mon Upper, Tue Lower, Thu Upper, Fri Lower).
upper_lower_4x_week.upper_a: Barbell Bench Press → Barbell Row → Overhead Press → Lat Pulldown → Lateral Raise → Tricep Pushdown → Barbell Curl → Face Pull
upper_lower_4x_week.upper_b: Incline Dumbbell Press → Dumbbell Row → Dumbbell Shoulder Press → Cable Row → Rear Delt Flye → Overhead Extension → Hammer Curl → Face Pull
upper_lower_4x_week.lower_a: Barbell Back Squat → Romanian Deadlift → Leg Press → Leg Curl → Walking Lunge → Calf Raise → Cable Crunch
upper_lower_4x_week.lower_b: Trap Bar Deadlift → Bulgarian Split Squat → Hip Thrust → Leg Extension → Leg Curl → Calf Raise → Hanging Leg Raise
upper_lower_4x_week.superset_opportunities: Bench ↔ Row, OHP ↔ Pulldown, Curls ↔ Pushdowns, Leg Extension ↔ Leg Curl
upper_lower_4x_week.notes: A/B variants provide exercise variety across the week while hitting the same muscle groups.

push_pull_legs_6x_week.description: Popular advanced split. Each session focuses on one movement type. Run twice per week (Push A, Pull A, Legs A, Push B, Pull B, Legs B).
push_pull_legs_6x_week.push: Bench Press → Incline Press → Shoulder Press → Lateral Raise → Cable Flye → Tricep Pushdown → Overhead Extension
push_pull_legs_6x_week.pull: Pull-Up or Pulldown → Barbell Row → Cable Row → Face Pull → Barbell Curl → Hammer Curl → Rear Delt Flye
push_pull_legs_6x_week.legs: Squat → Romanian Deadlift → Leg Press → Leg Curl → Lunge variation → Leg Extension → Calf Raise → Abs
push_pull_legs_6x_week.superset_opportunities: Within push: lateral raise ↔ tricep pushdown. Within pull: face pull ↔ curls. Within legs: leg extension ↔ leg curl. Core exercises between heavy compounds.
push_pull_legs_6x_week.notes: Complements arrays are most useful here — all exercises in a push session should appear in each other's complement lists.

scientific_principles_simplified.exercise_order: Big compounds first, small isolations last. Maximizes safety and strength gains. This is reflected in the workout_position field.
scientific_principles_simplified.push_pull_balance: Match pushing volume with pulling volume across the week. Prevents imbalances and shoulder injuries. Face pulls and band pull-aparts should be in every program.
scientific_principles_simplified.compound_to_isolation: 60-70% compounds, 30-40% isolations for most people. Compounds build the most muscle per unit time.
scientific_principles_simplified.frequency: Hit each muscle 2x per week minimum. Better than 1x per week for growth (Schoenfeld et al., 2016). Full body and upper/lower splits naturally achieve this.
scientific_principles_simplified.progressive_overload: Gradually increase weight, reps, or sets over time. The #1 rule of getting stronger. Cable crunches and cable curls allow fine-grained loading increases.
scientific_principles_simplified.rep_ranges: 1-5 = strength focus. 6-12 = strength + size. 12-20 = endurance + size. All build muscle if effort is high enough (within ~3 reps of failure).
scientific_principles_simplified.rest_periods: Heavy compounds: 2-5 min. Moderate work: 90-120 sec. Isolations: 60-90 sec. Use rest periods for supersets (antagonist work or core filler).
scientific_principles_simplified.weekly_volume: 10-20 sets per muscle group per week. Beginners start at 10, advanced up to 20. Count compound sets toward all muscles they train.
scientific_principles_simplified.stretch_mediated_hypertrophy: Exercises that load muscles in their stretched position may produce superior growth (Maeo et al., 2021). Examples: incline curls for biceps, overhead extensions for triceps, deep squats for quads, RDLs for hamstrings.
scientific_principles_simplified.core_training_paradigms: The core functions through 6 distinct paradigms: spinal flexion (crunches), anti-extension (planks), anti-rotation (Pallof press), active rotation (woodchops), anti-lateral flexion (side planks, suitcase carries), and hip flexion (leg raises). A complete program trains at least 3-4 of these.
scientific_principles_simplified.unilateral_training: Single-limb exercises (Bulgarian split squats, single-arm rows, concentration curls) fix left/right imbalances and improve stability. Include at least one unilateral exercise per muscle group in your program.

graph_structure.overview: Each exercise is a node. Substitutes, complements, and superset_candidates are edges. The graph has 162 nodes and 1340 edges (516 substitute, 513 complement, 311 superset).
graph_structure.edge_weighting: Weight edges by similarity: same movement_pattern + same primary_muscles = strongest substitute. Same force_type + same workout_position = strongest complement. Opposite force_type + non-competing muscles = strongest superset.
graph_structure.cross_file_edges: 434 edges cross file boundaries. These are critical for generating multi-muscle-group workouts. Most cross-file edges are in superset_candidates (antagonist pairs) and complements (synergist links from arms to their compound partners).

workout_generation: 1. User picks template (full body, upper/lower, PPL) — determines which muscle groups appear in a session
2. Use muscle_groups.antagonist to derive which groups pair together for upper/lower and full body splits
3. Pick 1-2 'early' position compounds for the session's primary muscle groups
4. Add 1-2 'mid' position compounds via complement edges from the primary exercises
5. Fill 2-4 'late' position isolations for muscles that need more volume — traverse complement edges
6. If equipment unavailable, traverse 'substitutes' edges for replacements (same pattern guaranteed)
7. Optionally pair exercises as supersets using superset_candidates for time efficiency
8. Validate: push/pull balanced across the week, correct position order within session, 15-25 total sets per session
superset_generation: 1. For each exercise in the session, check its superset_candidates
2. If any candidate is ALSO in the session, suggest that pairing first (most convenient)
3. If no candidates are in the session, suggest adding a superset partner from the candidates list
4. Prefer antagonist pairs (bench ↔ row) over non-competing fillers (plank between squats)
5. Core exercises make excellent fillers between any compound — their superset_candidates point to major compounds
equipment_filtering: 1. User specifies available equipment (home gym vs commercial gym)
3. For filtered-out exercises, follow substitute edges to find available alternatives
4. If no substitute is available either, drop the slot and redistribute sets to remaining exercises
beginner_mode: Default to 'beginner' difficulty exercises only (124 of 162 exercises)
Show video_url prominently for every exercise
Display beginner_tips inline during the workout
Suggest progression_chains when user can complete all sets at the top of the rep range
Cap total sets at 15-18 per session (3 sets of 5-6 exercises)
Always include face_pull or band_pull_apart in every session for shoulder health
